title: Obra Maestra
primary_topics: SC decisions as literature, philosophy, history
sub_topics: Centenary Reader Volume 1: Supreme Court Decisions as Literature
Centenary Reader Volume 2: Supreme Court Decisions as Philosophy
Centenary Reader Volume 3: Supreme Court Decisions as History
twofold SC responsibility (reversible errors + grave abuse of discretion)
Article VIII Section 1 of the 1987 Constitution
Avelino v. Cuenco (1949) — judicial-restraint critique by J. Perfecto
Santiago v. Guingona (1998) — Panganiban ponencia on Senate minority leader
political question doctrine's narrowed reach under 1987 Constitution
'4 Cs' of effective legal writing (correct, complete, clear, concise)
Francisco v. Permskul (1989) — 'pride of authorship' and 'little style'
Morales v. Enrile (1983) quotation on judgment of history

stances.claim: The Supreme Court has a twofold responsibility: first to review lower court decisions and correct reversible errors; second, to pass upon acts of any branch or instrumentality of government to determine grave abuse of discretion 'amounting to lack or excess of jurisdiction.'
stances.rhetorical_move: structural-doctrinal restatement of Article VIII Section 1

stances.claim: When the Constitution is violated by a political branch (Presidency or Congress), the SC shall come in to strike down the transgression — not because it has any superior-inferior relationship with the violator, but because 'the Constitution must be upheld as truly paramount.'

The 1987 Constitution has narrowed the reach of the political question doctrine — by expressly vesting in the judiciary the power to determine grave abuse of discretion, it has expanded judicial prerogatives beyond the older Avelino v. Cuenco framework.

Judicial 'hands-off' policy is 'in effect, a showing of official inferiority complex' — premised on notions of reality fundamentally wrong, like its psychological parallel (Justice Perfecto in Avelino, with 'courage admirable in those days of judicial restraint').
stances.rhetorical_move: Perfecto-citation + psychological metaphor for judicial passivity critique

stances.claim: Decision-writing should follow the 4 Cs — correct, complete, clear, concise; kilometric decisions and excessive verbiage are to be avoided, but so is the opposite extreme; 'the ideal decision is that which, with welcome economy of words, arrives at the factual findings, reaches the legal conclusion, renders its ruling and, having done so, ends.'

A judicial decision does not have to be a bore — the SC itself extols 'pride of authorship' and exhorts members to use 'a little style' to 'help liven the opinion trapped in the tortuous lexicon of the law with all its whereases and wherefores.'
stances.rhetorical_move: Francisco-v-Permskul citation + literary-craft framing of judicial writing

stances.claim: The Centenary Reader excerpts capture the SC's transformation from 'a passive, laid back, generally cautious and conservative evaluator of past dramas' to 'a proactive, interacting and courageous mover and moulder of current and future events.'

'When the judgment of history is written, as leaders of our people, we shall be asked to account not only for what we did, not only for what we did not do, but also for what visions we have today of our tomorrow.' (Morales v. Enrile)
stances.rhetorical_move: structural-accountability triad (did / did not do / envisioned)

events: Publication of the Centenary Reader trilogy

signature_phrases: the 4 Cs of effective legal writing
a judicial decision does not have to be a bore
tortuous lexicon of the law with all its whereases and wherefores
Judicial 'hands-off' policy is, in effect, a showing of official inferiority complex
courage admirable in those days of judicial restraint
a proactive, interacting and courageous mover and moulder of current and future events
what visions we have today of our tomorrow
register_markers: verbatim case quotation with footnoted citations
rhetorical question device ('which agency or mechanism would be imbued with the power…')
decision_framework_signals: applies twofold-judicial-role framework (reversible errors + grave abuse of discretion) as constitutional architecture
uses constitutional-supremacy doctrine (Court as guardian, not superior) to legitimize judicial review
frames 1987 Constitution as expanding judicial power vs older political-question doctrine
frames judicial-restraint as 'official inferiority complex' (Perfecto citation) for proactive jurisprudence advocacy
uses Morales-v-Enrile triad (did / did not do / envisioned) for institutional accountability
cross_references: Estrada v. Desierto referenced as 'leading example' of decision as literature/philosophy/history
notable_anecdotes: Justice Perfecto's concurring opinion in Avelino v. Cuenco — 'with courage admirable in those days of judicial restraint' — declaring 'Judicial hands-off policy is, in effect, a showing of official inferiority complex'
His own ponencia in Santiago v. Guingona (November 1998) taking jurisdiction over Senate-minority-leader controversy, 'roundly accused of interfering in an internal act of the Senate'
